name: Projective Geometry & the Cross-Ratio
whatIsIt: PROJECTIVE GEOMETRY studies what stays true when you PROJECT a picture (like a shadow from a point). Lengths and simple ratios change, but the CROSS-RATIO of four collinear points is INVARIANT. For points A, B, C, D on a line it is (A,B;C,D) = (AC/BC)·(BD/AD). A second big idea: parallel lines are treated as meeting at a single 'POINT AT INFINITY', so every two lines meet.
whyNeeded: The cross-ratio is the one number a projection can't distort, which makes it a master key for Olympiad problems about lines, conics, and perspective — and it explains why parallel rails appear to meet on the horizon.
realLifeUses: Perspective drawing and vanishing points in art

vocabulary.meaning: Casting points onto another line from a fixed viewpoint.
(AC/BC)·(BD/AD) for four collinear points — unchanged by projection.
The single point where a family of parallel lines is said to meet.

story.title: The Railway Lines
story.text: Kavya notices the railway tracks seem to meet far away, though they're parallel. Dadu smiles: 'In projective geometry, parallel lines meet at a point at infinity — that meeting point is the horizon's vanishing point.' Then he marks four posts and photographs them from an angle. 'The spacings look different in the photo, but one number — the cross-ratio — is exactly the same.' Kavya wonders: 'So projection hides lengths but keeps the cross-ratio?' 'Precisely.'
story.extractedProblem: In projective geometry, where do two parallel lines meet?
story.answerInStory: At a single point at infinity — the vanishing point you see where parallel rails appear to converge.

props.label: Projecting A,B,C,D from a point keeps the cross-ratio (AC/BC)(BD/AD)

visual.caption: Projection from a point changes lengths and simple ratios, but the cross-ratio of four collinear points stays exactly the same.

props.label: Same four points, two lines: equal cross-ratio
Parallel lines meet at a point at infinity (vanishing point)

standardMethod.summary: For four collinear points, compute the cross-ratio (A,B;C,D) = (AC/BC)·(BD/AD) using signed lengths. Because projection preserves it, equate the cross-ratio before and after projecting to solve the problem. Treat parallel lines as meeting at a point at infinity.
steps: Order the four collinear points and note their positions.
Use that projection keeps this value the same on any image line.
For parallels, use the point at infinity so every two lines meet.

workedExamples.problem: Points on a line at A = 0, B = 2, C = 3, D = 6. Find the cross-ratio (A,B;C,D) = (AC/BC)·(BD/AD).
In projective geometry, how many points do two distinct parallel lines share?
steps: Parallel lines meet at a point at infinity.
That is exactly one shared point.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking ordinary distances or simple ratios survive projection.
commonMistakes.fix: Projection distorts lengths and simple ratios; only the CROSS-ratio is invariant.
In projective geometry parallel lines meet at a single point at infinity — so any two lines meet.

Two parallel lines in projective geometry…

options.label: Meet at a point at infinity

questions.answer: Meet at a point at infinity

teachBackPrompt: Use railway tracks with a grown-up. Explain that parallel lines appear to meet at a vanishing point — the 'point at infinity' — and that when you photograph four posts from an angle the spacings change but one number, the cross-ratio (AC/BC)·(BD/AD), stays exactly the same.

revisionCard.summary: Projective geometry: projection preserves the CROSS-RATIO (A,B;C,D) = (AC/BC)·(BD/AD) of four collinear points, though lengths and simple ratios change. Parallel lines meet at a single point at infinity (the vanishing point), so any two lines meet.

rememberIt.hook: Projection keeps the cross-ratio; parallels meet at infinity

studentQuestions.q: Why is only the cross-ratio preserved?
studentQuestions.a: A projection is a fractional-linear map on the line; such maps stretch lengths and ratios but leave the cross-ratio — a ratio of ratios — unchanged.
Four points with cross-ratio exactly −1; they show up in many Olympiad configurations (poles, polars, and the diagonals of complete quadrilaterals).

formulas.name: Projective meeting
formulas.formula: any two distinct lines meet in exactly 1 point
